problem_summary: 2024 MCM Problem A (continuous): model the population dynamics of sea lampreys, whose juvenile-growth-rate-dependent sex differentiation lets the sex ratio shift with the environment. Tasks: (1) model lamprey growth/reproduction with variable vs. fixed sex ratio; (2) advantages/disadvantages of variable sex ratio to the population itself; (3) impact on ecosystem stability; (4) whether variable sex ratio benefits other species in the ecosystem. This team built a sex- and age-structured discrete population model ("BFM Model") and coupled it to predator/host/parasite sub-models, then used control-theory feedback analysis and quantitative stability/diversity indices to answer all four tasks.
models_used: BFM Model: a custom 3-compartment (juvenile B, adult female F, adult male M) sex- and age-structured population model, built by extending the Logistic growth model with a sex differentiation ratio alpha and a juvenile development time tau
Logistic population growth model as the base for juvenile dynamics (carrying capacity K)

Difference-equation (discretized) formulation with 1-month time step to capture annual/seasonal cyclicity, including a delay/age-structure term where maturation at month t sums over cohorts born at month i satisfying t = i + tau_i
Sex-separated 'Advanced Lotka-Volterra' predator-prey model (lampreys as prey vs. Esox lucius), with sex-specific predation difficulty (epsilon_F, epsilon_M) and energy yield (theta_F, theta_M)

Nicholson-Bailey host-parasitoid model used two ways: lampreys-as-parasite on Crucian carp host (H_{t+1}=r_H H_t e^{-(beta_F F + beta_M M)}), and Lepeophtheirus parasites on lampreys (J_{t+1}=F(1-e^{-gamma_F J})+M(1-e^{-gamma_M J}))
Linear empirical sub-models calibrated by data fitting: alpha = c*tau + d (c=-0.009, d=0.764) and tau = k*B + b (k=-0.000844, b=35.678), derived from an energy-accumulation argument
Control-theory Feedback Model: positive-feedback loop (constant alpha) vs. negative-feedback loop (variable alpha), explaining stability via self-excited oscillation vs. limit-cycle behavior
Sinusoidal-plus-impulse mortality model mu_t = A0 + A1*sin(2pi/T) + A2*delta(t-t0) to inject seasonal and shock perturbations
Simpson Diversity Index (D = 1 - sum p_j^2) and Shannon-Wiener Index (H = -sum p_j log2 p_j) as biodiversity metrics

Custom ecosystem-stability functionals: resistance S1 = sup|f1-f2| (L-infinity / Chebyshev distance between perturbed and baseline curves), resilience S2 = recovery time, comprehensive stability S = integral of |f1-f2| dt (area between curves)
key_techniques: Reformulating a multi-task open prompt into a dependency-ordered solving plan: they explicitly noted Tasks 1 and 4 are coupled and answered in order 1, 4, 2, 3 rather than sequentially
Building ONE core model (BFM) and reusing it across every task by swapping the alpha/tau terms between constants and time-varying functions, then plugging it into interaction sub-models

Mechanistic derivation of the tau-B linear law from an energy-budget inequality (accumulate fixed energy W at efficiency eta until maturation), then simplifying via the steady-state approximation B_t ~ const for t>100
Sex-disaggregation of every interaction coefficient (epsilon, theta, beta, gamma) to make the 'sex ratio matters' thesis mechanistically active rather than decorative

Using control theory (positive vs. negative feedback loops, self-excited oscillation, limit cycle) as the EXPLANATORY layer for why variable sex ratio confers stability, backed by F-B-t phase-trajectory plots showing a 'trumpet' (diverging) vs. converging limit cycle

Quantifying the vague word 'stability' three distinct ways (resistance = max deviation, resilience = recovery time, comprehensive = area between curves) plus biodiversity indices, giving numeric tables (Table 3, Table 4) instead of hand-waving

Honest counter-finding: defining a breeding success rate G = Rate_F * Rate_M and showing variable sex ratio LOWERS it (0.2114 vs 0.2467), then tying it to lampreys being evolutionary 'living fossils' (Fisher's sex-ratio theory)

Turning sensitivity analysis into management policy: sweeping r_H, K, and mortality parameters A0/A1/A2 to map onto real control levers (TFM lampricide reduces K; continuous fishing = A0; seasonal chemical control = A1; concentrated culling = A2), and finding that mass culling timed to the female-population minimum is most effective
why_it_won: "It answered all four tasks with a single coherent, custom-built mechanistic model rather than bolting on disconnected sub-models, and the sex ratio was load-bearing everywhere (sex-specific predation/parasitism coefficients, the alpha-tau-B feedback chain). Judges reward a model that is clearly the team's own. The standout move is layering EXPLANATION on top of simulation: the control-theory feedback model plus phase-trajectory plots explain WHY variable sex ratio stabilizes the population (negative feedback -> limit cycle) rather than just showing curves that differ. It quantified the famously vague notion of 'ecosystem stability' three rigorous ways and reported actual numbers, satisfying the modeling-and-results bar. It demonstrated intellectual honesty by surfacing a genuine disadvantage (lower breeding success rate) and connecting it to a real biological fact (lampreys as living fossils), which signals deep engagement. Finally, the sensitivity analysis was converted directly into actionable, mechanism-grounded invasion-control recommendations (TFM, timed culling), giving the paper real-world payoff that judges look for in a complete report."
section_highlights: "Summary clearly names the model (BFM) and front-loads all four task conclusions in plain language. The 'Our Work' flowchart (Figure 2) maps the entire solution architecture at a glance. Assumptions are each paired with a literature-cited justification (papers [3]-[5]), not asserted. The deliberate task-reordering note (1,4,2,3) shows the team thought about dependencies. The Feedback Model section is the intellectual core: it pairs schematic feedback diagrams (positive vs. negative) with F-B-t phase-trajectory 'trumpet vs. limit cycle' visualizations, giving both qualitative and quantitative explanation. Tables 2-4 deliver crisp numeric comparisons (G, S1/S2/S, Simpson/Shannon) of constant vs. variable alpha. The sensitivity section explicitly maps each parameter to a real control strategy. The Model Evaluation honestly flags the feedback model as only 'semi-quantitative' and proposes the exact follow-up (compute feedback coefficients from the differential dynamical system)."
pitfalls_or_limitations: The feedback model is admittedly semi-quantitative: positive/negative feedback is asserted from loop topology and matched to simulation shapes, never proven via an actual stability/Jacobian analysis or computed feedback coefficients (the team flags this themselves in Weaknesses)

The two key calibration laws alpha=c*tau+d and tau=k*B+b rest on single secondary-source fits and an idealized energy-budget derivation that assumes B_t is constant after t>100; parameter provenance is thin and not independently validated

Plots are simulation outputs with no real lamprey time-series data fitted or validated against, so claims like 'coevolution' and 'coexistence' are interpretations of the team's own simulation rather than empirical findings

Interaction sub-models are each single-species (one predator, one host, one parasite) and the BFM substitutes for Nicholson-Bailey on the host side, so the coupled multi-species ecosystem is simplified; biodiversity indices are computed over only ~4 species
The mortality impulse uses a Dirac delta delta(t-t0) inside a discrete difference equation, which is dimensionally/conceptually loose; effectively it's a one-step spike
Some result tables/figures show monotone or suspiciously linear trends (e.g., several sensitivity charts) suggesting illustrative/schematic plotting rather than raw simulation dumps
Resistance stability S1 defined as a sup-norm (single worst-case point) is sensitive to noise/outliers and can disagree with the area-based comprehensive measure S
reusable_patterns: Build ONE custom core model and reuse it across all tasks by toggling a single mechanism (here: constant vs. time-varying alpha/tau) - gives coherence and lets every task be a controlled A/B comparison
Name your model (BFM) and open the Summary with the model name plus all task conclusions in plain English
Reorder tasks by dependency and say so explicitly when tasks are coupled

Disaggregate interaction coefficients along the dimension your thesis depends on (here sex: epsilon_F vs epsilon_M, beta_F vs beta_M) so the central variable is mechanistically active everywhere, not just narrated
When the prompt uses a vague word ('stability', 'advantage'), define it 2-3 distinct quantitative ways and report a numeric comparison table
Add an EXPLANATORY layer (control-theory feedback loops + phase-trajectory plots) on top of simulation so you explain WHY, not just show that curves differ - a strong differentiator
Deliberately include an honest disadvantage backed by domain fact (breeding success rate down + 'living fossil') to demonstrate balanced, deep analysis

Convert sensitivity analysis directly into policy by mapping each parameter to a real-world control lever (K<->lampricide TFM, A0/A1/A2<->continuous/seasonal/concentrated fishing), so the math yields actionable recommendations
Derive empirical sub-laws from a first-principles argument (energy-budget inequality) then simplify with a justified steady-state approximation, rather than fitting a black-box curve
Discretize a continuous ODE model to a monthly difference equation when the system has natural seasonal cyclicity, making perturbation injection and age-structure delays trivial to implement
State each assumption with a cited justification immediately beneath it
Use a single 'Our Work' architecture flowchart so judges grasp the whole solution structure at a glance
